attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with three weeks of progressive shortness of breath, facial swelling, and prominent neck veins. He reports drenching night sweats and has lost 8 kg over the past two months. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. There is marked facial plethora and dilated superficial veins over his chest wall. When his arms are raised above his head for 60 seconds, he develops increasing facial plethora and cyanosis. He has several firm, non-tender lymph nodes in his left supraclavicular fossa. Chest X-ray reveals a large anterior mediastinal mass.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the physical sign demonstrated?
attributes.explanation:
The physical finding described in this case is Pemberton's sign, which occurs when raising the arms above the head causes increased facial plethora and cyanosis. This sign indicates superior vena cava (SVC) obstruction, as raising the arms forces a retrosternal mass downward, compressing the SVC and causing venous congestion of the face and neck.

Cullen's sign refers to periumbilical bruising, typically seen in acute pancreatitis or ruptured ectopic pregnancy. It is caused by tracking of hemorrhagic fluid through the falciform ligament.

Kussmaul's sign is a paradoxical rise in jugular venous pressure during inspiration, classically seen in constrictive pericarditis or right ventricular failure.

Grey Turner's sign describes bruising in the flanks, another sign of retroperitoneal hemorrhage often seen in severe acute pancreatitis.

Trousseau's sign refers to spontaneous carpopedal spasm induced by occluding blood flow to the arm with a blood pressure cuff, indicating hypocalcaemia.

Recognition of Pemberton's sign is crucial as it indicates significant mediastinal pathology causing SVC obstruction, which requires urgent investigation and management. The underlying cause in this case is likely lymphoma given the patient's age, B symptoms, and anterior mediastinal mass.
learningPoints: Pemberton's sign (facial plethora and cyanosis when arms are raised) indicates superior vena cava obstruction.
Superior vena cava obstruction commonly presents with facial and neck swelling, dilated chest wall veins, and dyspnoea.
Anterior mediastinal masses causing SVC obstruction require urgent investigation and treatment.
references: De Filippis EA, Sabet A, Sun MRM, Garber JR. Pemberton’s Sign: Explained Nearly 70 Years Later. The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism. 2014 Jun 1;99(6):1949–54.
